basics.name: Sachin Sagar
basics.title: Embedded Applications Engineer (ADAS)
basics.summary:
I am a mechanical engineer by degree, an embedded applications developer by profession, and a relentless builder at heart. I thrive at the intersection of hardware and software, specializing in perception systems and bringing intelligence to the edge. I am of the opinion: the harder the challenge, the greater the joy.

In my professional work, I focus heavily on the Texas Instruments ecosystem, developing perception algorithms for mmWave radars. My work involves writing and debugging embedded C on the onboard microprocessors using Code Composer Studio, as well as meticulously tuning radar configurations to extract the highest quality and density of point clouds. Discipline and adaptability are my greatest assets, allowing me to dive headfirst into complex, cross-functional projects and figure out exactly what it takes to ship them.

My drive to build extends beyond working hours. I spend my evenings and weekends expanding skills in Edge AI and hardware integration. I like tinkering with microcontrollers, deploying machine learning models to resource-constrained devices, and building out real-time sensor pipelines from scratch, I relish the process of creating something from nothing.

I like to start my mornings prioritizing my health at the gym. Off the clock, you can find me taking long motorcycle rides or lost in fantasy books at coffee shops.

bulletPoints: Developed the company's first end-to-end ADAS perception stack on the TI AWR1843 platform, and established tools for CI/CD workflows.

Architected real-time sensor fusion on the onboard DSP, synchronizing IMU data, and vehicle odometry from the vehicle CAN bus with radar point clouds for clustering (DBSCAN) and tracking (JPDA). Designed an Interacting Mixture Model (IMM).
Programmed and debugged the firmware using Code Composer Studio (CCS) within a FreeRTOS environment on AWR1843/1432 SoCs.
Engineered a comprehensive web-based visualization suite using JavaScript to synchronize and replay Radar, Camera, and CAN log data.
Implemented active safety features such as Active Emergency Braking and Adaptive Cruise Control using track collision probability.
Deployed a sensor-less mass and grade estimation algorithm for a shared mobility project using existing CAN bus data.
Engineered a custom Coordinate Transformation library in MATLAB to normalize raw IMU readings from the sensor frame to the vehicle frame across multiple SKUs.
Used the processed IMU data for firmware-level IMU applications (roll-over detection, blinker auto-cancellation, pitch-based regen/hill-hold) to the vehicle's production software.
Resolved 20+ critical firmware defects in traction control and hill-assist logic through rigorous unit testing and system-level debugging.
Leading the design team of the accumulator and thermal management sub-systems.
Senior powertrain engineer under HV, motor, and control.
Leading the thermal design of drivetrain and accumulator. Working on motor and control.
Worked on cell chemistry analysis, heat load estimation, data acquisition system, and lap-time simulation software.

Validated engineering designs against real-world test data to ensure performance reliability.
Achievement: Optimized simulation workflows, improving the accuracy of thermal load predictions.

projects.name: Road-Anomaly Detection & Geospatial Mapping
bulletPoints: Developed an Edge AI road-anomaly detection system using STM32 X-CUBE-AI.
Integrated 6-axis IMU data for high-frequency vibration analysis.
projects.blog:
This project focuses on identifying road anomalies (like potholes, bumps, or uneven surfaces) in real-time using Edge AI. The system is built around an STM32 microcontroller and utilizes the X-CUBE-AI expansion package to deploy a neural network model directly onto the edge device. By analyzing high-frequency vibration data from a 6-axis IMU, the model can accurately classify different types of road irregularities. 

The motivation behind this project was to create a low-cost, scalable solution for road maintenance monitoring. Instead of relying on expensive specialized vehicles, this system can be deployed on regular commuter vehicles or delivery fleets to continuously crowdsource road quality data.
results: Achieved 92% classification accuracy on the edge device.
Inference time of less than 5ms, allowing for real-time processing.
Successfully mapped over 50km of local roads during testing.

projects.name: Custom Embedded C Libraries
bulletPoints: Implemented optimized Radix-2 Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and Quaternion-based 3D rotation libraries from scratch.
Designed for resource-constrained ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers with a focus on zero dynamic memory allocation.
projects.blog:
This project is a collection of high-performance, bare-metal C libraries designed for real-time embedded systems. 

1. **Real-time FFT**: A highly optimized Radix-2 Fast Fourier Transform algorithm. By implementing bit-reversal sorting, pre-computed twiddle factor tables, and eliminating function call overhead, the library enables spectral analysis on low-power microcontrollers with sub-millisecond execution times.

2. **Quaternion Kinematics**: A coordinate rotation library using unit quaternions. Quaternions provide a computationally efficient and robust alternative to Euler angles, completely avoiding 'Gimbal Lock' and simplifying the composition of multiple rotations. The implementation uses a specialized vector rotation formula to minimize the number of multiplications per transformation.
results: Execution time: < 1ms for a 1024-point FFT on Cortex-M4.
Quaternion rotation: 40% faster than standard Euler-based matrix transformations.
Zero heap usage: Entirely deterministic memory footprint for safety-critical applications.
Successfully integrated into a real-time IMU-based attitude estimation system.

projects.name: GPU Accelerated Clustering
bulletPoints: Developed a parallelized DBSCAN clustering algorithm using CUDA.
Significantly accelerated point-cloud processing for ADAS applications.
projects.blog:
Clustering large, dense point clouds generated by modern automotive sensors (like high-resolution radar and LiDAR) can be extremely computationally expensive. To tackle this bottleneck, I developed a GPU-accelerated version of the DBSCAN clustering algorithm using CUDA.

By parallelizing the neighborhood search and cluster expansion phases, the algorithm can process hundreds of thousands of points simultaneously. The project involved deep dives into GPU architecture, optimizing memory coalescing, and minimizing Host-to-Device data transfer overheads to ensure that the parallelization benefits weren't lost to latency.
results: Achieved a 50x speedup over a standard CPU-based implementation.
Successfully processed point clouds with over 100,000 points in under 10ms.
Developed a reusable CUDA library for future perception tasks.

projects.name: Chaos Theory Simulations
bulletPoints: Developed a hardware-accelerated 2D plotting engine using SFML in C++.
Implemented interactive Bifurcation Diagrams and Lorenz Attractors with constant point density zooming.
projects.blog: An interactive, object-oriented C++ visualization tool for exploring chaotic dynamical systems and non-linear dynamics. Inspired by James Gleick's *Chaos*, this project features a custom-built, hardware-accelerated 2D plotting library using SFML. The suite includes simulations for the Logistic Map Bifurcation Diagram and the Lorenz Attractor, utilizing 4th-Order Runge-Kutta (RK4) numerical integration for physical accuracy.
results: Built a generalized 2D plotting engine capable of rendering massive datasets using Vertex Arrays.
Implemented smooth interactive panning and infinite zoom with constant point density.
Achieved high-performance real-time simulation using -O3 optimized C++ loops.

education.notes: Was awarded a minor in Systems Engineering for completing 4 graduate-level courses in estimation, controls, optimization, and ML
